title: Why being asked to sit still triggers panic in some survivors
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
The stillness that isn't still at all 

 I remember the first time a yoga teacher told me to close my eyes and sit with my breath. My heart started hammering....
metaDescription:
The stillness that isn't still at all 

 I remember the first time a yoga teacher told me to close my eyes and sit with my breath. My heart started hammer...
htmlContent:
The stillness that isn't still at all

I remember the first time a yoga teacher told me to close my eyes and sit with my breath. My heart started hammering. My chest tightened. I felt like I was going to crawl out of my skin. Everyone else in the room looked peaceful, serene even. And I was sitting there fighting a full-blown panic attack over something as simple as being still.

Here's the thing. I am not alone in this. So many trauma survivors I talk to describe the same experience. Meditation classes that feel like torture chambers. Breathing exercises that make them feel worse. Being told to "just relax" and feeling their nervous system scream NO.

It makes sense when you understand what is actually happening. Your body is not broken. It is working exactly as it was designed to work. And that design includes a survival system that does not know the difference between a predator and a yoga mat.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, explained this better than anyone. He showed that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. This happens below conscious awareness. Your body makes a decision about whether you are safe long before your brain catches up.

When you have a history of trauma, your nervous system gets calibrated differently. It learns that the world is not safe. It learns that people are not safe. And it learns that being still and quiet is actually one of the most dangerous things you can do.

Think about it. If you grew up in a home where silence meant something bad was about to happen, stillness becomes a threat cue. If you were hurt by someone who told you to sit still and be quiet, your body remembers that. It remembers the association between stillness and danger. And it will do everything in its power to get you out of that situation.

Wild, right? Your body is trying to protect you from a threat that no longer exists. But it does not know that. It only knows what it learned.

The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is Bessel van der Kolk's work on how trauma lives in the body. He explains this exact phenomenon with research and compassion.

Why your brain says freeze is not an option

There is a reason why sitting still feels like an emergency. It is because your survival brain learned that freezing was the worst possible response.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about the freeze response as a natural part of the defense cascade. When you cannot fight and you cannot flee, your body goes into freeze. This is a survival strategy. It is what happens when a mouse is caught by a cat and goes limp. It is what happens when a child cannot escape abuse and dissociates.

But here is the problem. If your freeze response was activated during trauma and you never got to complete the cycle, your body stays stuck. It remembers freeze as a trap. It remembers freeze as the moment when you were helpless. And it will avoid anything that feels like that state at all costs.

So when you are asked to sit still, your body does not hear "relax." It hears "go back to the place where you were powerless." And it screams NO.

I have had clients tell me they feel like they are going to die when they try to meditate. That is not an exaggeration. Their nervous system is interpreting stillness as a life-threatening situation. Because for them, at some point in their history, stillness meant death or violation.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who works with structural dissociation, describes this as parts of the self that carry the trauma. There is a part that knows stillness is dangerous. There is a part that will do anything to keep you moving, keep you vigilant, keep you alive. That part is not your enemy. It is your protector.

Honestly, I think we need to stop pathologizing this response. It is not a defect. It is not a failure of your meditation practice. It is a sign that your survival system is working exactly as it should. It is just working based on old information.

Sit with that. Your body is not wrong. It is just trying to keep you safe based on what it learned.

The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is written by Arielle Schwartz. It walks through these exact experiences with exercises that respect your nervous system's limits.

What the wellness industry gets wrong about stillness

The wellness industry has a lot to answer for. It tells us that if we just meditate enough, do enough yoga, breathe enough, we will heal. And if we cannot do those things, the implication is that we are not trying hard enough.

This is toxic positivity dressed up in yoga pants. It is harmful. It shames people for having a normal biological response to trauma.

Look, I am not saying meditation is bad. I am not saying breathing exercises are useless. But they are not one-size-fits-all tools. And for trauma survivors, they can actually make things worse if not adapted properly.

Gabor Mate, who studies trauma and addiction, talks about how trauma is not what happens to you but what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you. The same is true for healing. It is not about what practice you do. It is about what happens inside you when you do it.

If sitting still triggers panic, you do not need to force yourself to sit still. You need to find a different way in. Maybe that means moving meditation. Walking. Dancing. Rocking. Anything that allows your body to feel safe while still being present.

Deb Dana, who applies Polyvagal Theory in clinical practice, emphasizes the importance of finding your own glimmers. These are small moments of safety that your nervous system can register. For some people, that glimmer comes from movement. For others, it comes from sound or touch or connection. The key is finding what works for your unique nervous system, not what some influencer on Instagram says you should be doing.

I have stopped forcing myself into stillness. When I feel the panic rising, I move. I stand up. I walk around. I shake my hands. I do whatever my body needs to feel safe again. And you know what? That is healing too. That is honoring your nervous system instead of fighting it.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did you feel a sense of relief? Did your shoulders drop even slightly? That is your system telling you it needs permission to be where it is.

How to actually work with a nervous system that hates stillness

So what do you do if sitting still triggers panic? You start smaller. Much smaller. You do not jump into a 30-minute meditation. You start with three conscious breaths while standing up. Or you try orienting to your environment instead of closing your eyes.

Peter Levine calls this pendulation. You move between a resource and a charge. You find something that feels safe, even for a moment, and you let yourself feel that. Then you gently touch the edge of the discomfort. Then you come back to safety. Back and forth. Pendulating. This is how you teach your nervous system that it can handle the charge without needing to go into full survival mode.

Here is another approach. Try movement-based practices that incorporate breath and awareness without demanding stillness. Yoga that focuses on flowing sequences rather than held poses. Tai chi. Qi gong. Even just walking with awareness of your surroundings.

Right? You can be present without being still. You can be mindful without meditating. You can heal without forcing yourself into a position that feels like death.

Another thing that helped me was understanding that my resistance to stillness was not a character flaw. It was a trauma response. And once I understood that, I could stop judging myself and start working with my system instead of against it.

I started by just noticing. When I felt the urge to move, I would notice it without judging it. I would say to myself, "Oh, there is the part that needs to move. That makes sense." And then I would move. I would get up and walk around. I would stretch. I would do whatever my body needed.

Over time, something shifted. My nervous system started to learn that I was paying attention. That I was listening. That I was not going to force it into a state it could not handle. And slowly, very slowly, it started to trust me a little more.

Running on Empty (paid link) - For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Jonice Webb's book. It explains how being forced to suppress your needs creates a deep sense of wrongness that shows up in your body.

It Didn't Start with You (paid link) - If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in Mark Wolynn's work on how family history shapes your nervous system responses.

The real goal is not stillness. It is safety.

I want to be clear about something. The goal is not to force yourself to sit still. The goal is to find a way to be present in your body that feels safe enough to tolerate. And for some people, that looks nothing like traditional meditation.

For me, it looks like sitting on the floor with my back against a wall, my eyes open, my hands resting on my thighs, and a weighted blanket on my lap. It looks like five minutes, not thirty. It looks like stopping when I feel the first hint of panic instead of pushing through.

For you, it might look different. And that is okay. That is more than okay. That is exactly how it should be.

Your nervous system is not broken. It is not wrong. It is just trying to keep you alive based on what it learned. And the path to healing is not about overriding that system. It is about listening to it. Understanding it. And slowly, gently, teaching it that you are safe now.

Even if that teaching happens while you are standing up, moving, and breathing in your own way.

I will leave you with this. The next time someone tells you to sit still and it triggers panic, you do not have to force yourself through it. You can say no. You can honor your body's wisdom. You can find your own way in. And that is not failure. That is the most healing thing you can do.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Running on Empty (paid link) - Jonice Webb's book on understanding and healing from emotional neglect.
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's foundational work on trauma and the body.
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - Arielle Schwartz's practical workbook for C-PTSD survivors.
  • It Didn't Start with You (paid link) - Mark Wolynn's book on inherited family trauma patterns.

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
